name: Passerellidae
description: Passerellidae, the New World sparrows, are a large and diverse family of passerine birds found primarily in the Americas. They are seed-eating birds with conical bills, often with streaked brown plumage and melodious songs.

children.name: Zonotrichia
children.description: A genus of large, handsome sparrows with distinctive crown patterns, found across North America.

children.name: Zonotrichia leucophrys
children.description: The White-crowned Sparrow is a large sparrow with bold black-and-white crown stripes and a pink bill. It breeds across northern North America and winters throughout much of the United States.

children.name: Zonotrichia albicollis
children.description: The White-throated Sparrow has a distinctive white throat, yellow lores, and black-and-white crown stripes. It breeds in Canadian forests and winters across the eastern United States.

children.name: Melospiza
children.description: A genus of medium-sized, heavily streaked sparrows found across North America.

children.name: Melospiza melodia
children.description: The Song Sparrow is a brown, heavily streaked sparrow with a central breast spot and a rich, melodious song. It is one of the most widespread and variable sparrows in North America.

children.name: Melospiza lincolnii
children.description: A handsome sparrow with a finely streaked buffy breast, a grey face, and a distinctive white eye-ring. It breeds across northern North America and in western mountain ranges, wintering in the southern United States and Central America. It is secretive and often difficult to observe well.

children.name: Melospiza georgiana
children.description: A small sparrow of marshes and wetlands with a rich rufous crown, grey breast, and dark streaked back. It breeds across northern North America and winters in the southern and eastern United States. Its song is a slow, monotonous trill that is a characteristic sound of northern bogs.

children.name: Spizella
children.description: A genus of small, slender sparrows with rufous crowns and plain underparts, found across North America.

children.name: Spizella passerina
children.description: The Chipping Sparrow is a small, neat sparrow with a bright rufous crown, grey breast, and white supercilium. It is common in open woodlands and suburban areas across most of North America.

children.name: Junco
children.description: A genus of sparrows with grey or brown plumage and white outer tail feathers, found across North America.

children.name: Junco hyemalis
children.description: The Dark-eyed Junco is a variable sparrow with dark grey or brown plumage, pink bill, and white outer tail feathers. It is a common winter visitor across most of the United States.

children.name: Passerella
children.description: A genus of large, heavily built sparrows with variable rufous and grey plumage.

children.name: Passerella iliaca
children.description: The Fox Sparrow is a large, plump sparrow with heavy reddish-brown streaking and a spotted breast. It is a skulking bird that breeds in northern forests and winters across the southern United States.

children.name: Pooecetes
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the Vesper Sparrow, a long-bodied sparrow of open grasslands and fields across North America.

children.name: Pooecetes gramineus
children.description: A large, pale sparrow of open grasslands with a white eye-ring, white outer tail feathers, and a small chestnut patch on the shoulder. It sings a beautiful, melodic song reminiscent of a Song Sparrow but sweeter, often delivered from a fence post or wire.

children.name: Passerculus
children.description: A widespread genus of small, streaked sparrows found in grasslands across North America. The Savannah Sparrow complex shows remarkable variation across subspecies, some of which were once considered separate species.

children.name: Passerculus sandwichensis
children.description: A small, streaked sparrow with a yellowish stripe above the eye, a short notched tail, and pinkish legs. It breeds across grasslands and tundra from Alaska and Canada through the western United States, wintering in the southern half of the continent.

children.name: Ammodramus
children.description: A genus of chunky, short-tailed sparrows adapted to dense grasslands, found across North America. They are notoriously secretive and are more often heard than seen, but their insect-like songs carry far across open fields.

children.name: Ammodramus savannarum
children.description: A small, flat-headed sparrow with a short tail, a pale central crown stripe, and a distinctive buzzy, insect-like song that resembles a grasshopper. It inhabits dry grasslands and prairies across much of North America, wintering in the southern United States and Mexico.

children.name: Ammospiza
children.description: A genus of sparrows specialised for life in coastal salt marshes, tidal creeks, and brackish wetlands along the coasts of North America. They have distinctive, rather flat heads and stout bills adapted for a diet of small crustaceans and insects in addition to seeds.

children.name: Ammospiza nelsoni
children.description: A small, furtive sparrow of salt marshes with a buff-orange face and breast, grey nape, and heavily streaked back. It breeds in freshwater and brackish marshes of interior Canada and along the Atlantic coast, wintering in coastal marshes of the southeastern United States.

children.name: Ammospiza maritima
children.description: A pale greyish sparrow with a yellow patch before the eye, a long bill, and a short tail, confined to salt marshes along the Atlantic and Gulf coasts of the United States. It is vulnerable to habitat loss and sea-level rise, with the extinct Dusky Seaside Sparrow as a subspecies.

children.name: Pipilo
children.description: A genus of large, handsome sparrows known as towhees, characterised by long tails, stout bills, and striking plumage patterns. They are primarily ground-foraging birds that scratch loudly through leaf litter with both feet in a backwards hop.

children.name: Pipilo chlorurus
children.description: A distinctive towhee with an olive-green back, a rufous crown, a white throat, and a grey face and breast. It breeds in mountain shrublands of the western United States, wintering in the southwestern states and Mexico.

children.name: Pipilo maculatus
children.description: A striking towhee with a black hood and upperparts spotted with white, rufous flanks, and a white belly. It is common in brushy habitats across western North America, scratching noisily through leaf litter.

children.name: Pipilo erythrophthalmus
children.description: A large, boldly patterned towhee of eastern North America with a black hood and upperparts (male), rufous flanks, and a white belly. Its distinctive call sounds like 'drink your tea!' and it is a vigorous leaf-scratcher in forest undergrowth.

children.name: Melozone
children.description: A genus of medium-sized sparrows with plain brown plumage, found from the southwestern United States through Mexico to Central America. They have stout, conical bills and often inhabit dry, scrubby habitats.

children.name: Melozone crissalis
children.description: A large, plain brown towhee with a long tail, a buffy throat and vent, and a notably dull appearance. It is endemic to California and Baja California, where it is a common and conspicuous resident of chaparral, gardens, and parks.

children.name: Melozone fusca
children.description: A pale grey-brown towhee with a rusty crown and vent, a central breast spot, and a long tail. It is found in arid canyons and scrublands from the southwestern United States through Mexico.

children.name: Aimophila
children.description: A genus of medium-sized sparrows with long tails and rusty crowns, found in arid and semi-arid scrublands from the southwestern United States through Mexico and Central America.

children.name: Aimophila ruficeps
children.description: A neat, long-tailed sparrow with a bright rufous crown, a grey face and breast, and a white throat. It inhabits rocky hillsides and dry scrub from California and Texas through Mexico. It is shy and often stays hidden in dense brush.

children.name: Amphispiza
children.description: A small genus of pale, desert-dwelling sparrows with striking facial patterns. They are well adapted to arid environments and are characteristic birds of the deserts and dry scrublands of western North America.

children.name: Amphispiza bilineata
children.description: A handsome sparrow of arid scrublands with a distinctive black bib, white supercilium, and plain grey-brown upperparts. It is a characteristic bird of the Mojave, Sonoran, and Chihuahuan deserts, where it sings a simple, tinkling song from exposed perches.

children.name: Chondestes
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the Lark Sparrow, one of the most distinctive North American sparrows with a striking facial pattern of chestnut, black, and white. It has a long, rounded tail and a stout seed-cracking bill.

children.name: Chondestes grammacus
children.description: A large, striking sparrow with a chestnut, black, and white facial pattern, a white belly with a central dark spot, and white corners on the tail. It breeds in open grasslands and savannas across central and western North America, wintering in Mexico and the southern United States.
